merger_name: Online Education Services – South Australian Institute of Business and Technology

targets.name: South Australian Institute of Business and Technology Pty Ltd

anzsic_codes.name: Technical and Vocational Education and Training

merger_description:
Online Education Services Pty Ltd (**OES**) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in South Australian Institute of Business and Technology Pty Ltd (**SAIBT**).

OES provides end-to-end online education delivery services to higher education providers such as universities, including delivering online courses on behalf of higher education providers. OES provides teaching and other services related to these courses on behalf of the higher education providers, though the courses themselves are formally offered by the higher education provider, not OES, and the qualifications arising from the courses are awarded by the provider, not OES. OES does not directly offer any educational courses (other than indirectly through Swinburne University, which owns 20% of OES).

SAIBT is part of the Navitas Group. SAIBT has historically offered diploma qualifications intended to act as pathway qualifications to bachelor degrees offered by the University of South Australia. Following the merger of the University of South Australia and the University of Adelaide to form Adelaide University, SAIBT is teaching out its remaining students, not enrolling new students and will have no students at completion. That is, while OES is acquiring SAIBT the entity, at the completion date, SAIBT will have no students, will not be conducting any business, and will exist as a dormant ‘shell’ with regulatory approvals to operate higher education diploma qualifications.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Online Education Services Pty Ltd (OES) applied for a notification
waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of 100% of the share
capital in South Australian Institute of Business and Technology Pty
Ltd (SAIBT), as described in the transaction documents provided as
part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, OES, provides end-to-end online education delivery
services to higher education providers such as universities. This
includes delivering online courses on behalf of higher education
providers. OES does not currently possess the regulatory approvals
necessary to provide these courses directly to students (other than
indirectly through Swinburne University, which owns 20% of OES –
with the remaining 80% owned by Seek Growth Nominees Pty Ltd).
The target, SAIBT, is part of the Navitas Group. SAIBT has historically
offered face-to-face diploma qualifications in Adelaide intended to
act as pathway qualifications to bachelor degrees offered by the
University of South Australia. Following the merger of the University
of South Australia and the University of Adelaide to form Adelaide
University, SAIBT commenced transitioning its students to Eynesbury
College, another entity in the Navitas Group. SAIBT has ceased
enrolling new students and will have no students at completion of
the Acquisition. As such, on completion, OES will acquire SAIBT as a
dormant ‘shell’ with regulatory approvals to operate higher education
diploma qualifications.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. the Acquisition will not increase concentration in the supply
of diploma/pathway qualifications in Australia
b. the Acquisition will facilitate new entry by OES into the
supply of diploma/pathway qualifications in Australia
c. there are alternative suppliers of diploma/pathway
qualifications in Australia.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.
